content.full_text:
State of California                                                                                    Franchise Tax Board
                                                                                                                   audit problem areas
        Memorandum

To      :   Michael Bennett, Executive Director                        Date    : September 12, 1977
            Fair Political Practices Commission
            1100 K Street                                             File No.: M-1
            Sacramento, CA  95814


From    :   Robert C. Summers

Subject :   Political Reform Audit Division Audit Seminar
            May, 1977


            Our Political Reform Audit Division Audit Seminar Report brought
            forth some audit problem areas, questions and suggested solutions,
            three of which are attached.  I hope they will prove of interest
            to you as feedback from our audit staff.


                        [signature]

            Chief, Political Reform Audit Division

            Attachments

            cc:  Doug Dick
                 Dan Converse
                 Jim Turrini
                 Jim Stevens

State of California                                REC'D JU_21 1977 S.Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To : Seminar Committee                                Date : June 7, 1977

                                                      File No.: ELM PR:JR:en


From : Jerry Richard

Subject : Mass Mailings

        Audit Problems:

        1. Inadequate committee records in this area when other phases of
           recordkeeping are good.

        2. People involved in campaign can't remember details concerning
           disposition of printed matter and use of postal account.

        3. Description of items on printer's invoices provide little de-
           tail. Copies of items are usually not kept by the committee.

        4. It is difficult to reconcile printing bills with the postal
           account.

           A. Large printing orders are used at various times throughout
              the campaign rather than being mailed in a single batch.

           B. Bulk rate postage is by piece rather than by weight. A
              single mailing often contains items from two or three
              printers folded together.

           C. The use of stamps, meters or mailing services compounds
              the audit problems.

        Audit Procedures:

        1. As a preliminary step make a workpaper using a photocopy of
           list of items received by Commission. Add information from
           the copies of the individual items in the audit package:
           an additional description of the item and name of printer.

        2. Obtain transcript of postal account. Tie down at least the
           large mailings.

        3. Tie printer's invoices to the items listed on preliminary workpapers. Obtain copies of items from large orders which can't
           be reconciled.

Seminar Committee
June 7, 1977
Page 2


Suggested Solution:
     1. Recordkeeping regulations for printed matter and postal ex-
        pense should be established by the Commission.
        A. Copies of all printed items would be retained by the
           committee.
        B. The method of distribution of all printed items would be
           recorded.
        C. Items sent to the Commission would include a cover letter
           indicating the date mailed, quantity sent and the method
           of mailing.


[signature]
Auditor, Southern District
Political Reform Audit Division

State of California                                                    Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To  :  PRAD Seminar Committee                          Date  : May 24, 1977

                                                       File No.: JFink

From  :  John Fraser

Subject :  PRAD Auditor Conference Workshops

          The following is a summary of two workshop discussions held on May 19 and 20
during the PRAD auditor conference at Aerojet. Discussion centered on
problems auditors had encountered during (campaign) audits of mass mailings
and audit techniques useful in coping with those problems. As a result of
the discussions, a conclusion and recommendation was reached by consensus
of the group members.

Problems Encountered

     1. Reluctance of postal service employees to provide information
        regarding a bulk account.

     2. Committees do not keep useful records of mailings/cannot
        identify specific mailings to specific costs and printings.

     3. Audit time required to reconcile postal expenses to print-
        ings and to contact third parties is usually not justified
        by the resultant findings.

     4. Committee's use of stamps makes tracing mailings very
        difficult.

     5. Even when all printing costs and mailing costs are known,
        it is difficult, if not impossible, to identify specific
        mailing costs with copies of printings.

     6. Copies of mass mailings did not reach the San Francisco
        office until after some Committee audits were completed.

     7. FPPC writes to filers as to what types of mailings were
        required to be sent to that body was inconsistent. For
        example, one committee is told by the FPPC that a thank
        you letter must be sent to the Commission; another
        committee is told a fundraiser invitation needn't be
        sent.

Useful Audit Techniques

     1. Contacting third parties - postal service, committee volun-
        teers, printers, mailing houses, etc.

     2. Review FPPC mass mailing index.

PRAD Seminar Committee
May 24, 1977
Page 2

3. Utilizing experience of peers when possible.
4. Review confirmation letters for nonmonetary contributions
   (printing, postage).
5. Review FPPC opinions regarding mass mailings.
6. Determine existence of mimeograph use by committees (when
   postal expense exists without correlating printing cost).

Conclusion

Tracing printings to mailings is a time consuming procedure which may
not be justified by resultant findings, although all auditors agreed that
auditor judgement should dictate the extensiveness of this part of a campaign
audit.

Recommendation

Some minimal recordkeeping requirements for committees are needed and, if
not required by regulation, should at least be provided as Recordkeeping
Guidelines. Information desirable includes a copy of each mailing made,
date and quantity sent, cost, method of postage used. (Auditors agreed
that issuance of new regulations to this effect is unlikely since other
recordkeeping requirements are vague and not specific.

[signature]
Auditor
Audit Group 03

State of California                                                    Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To    :    Conference Committee                                   Date    :    May 24, 1977
          Mark Ford, Michele Holm, Jerry Richard
                                                                  File No.:    CJT:mc


From  :    Carmella J. Tamayo

Subject:   Fund Raisers


          Planning a fund raiser will usually correlate with the size of a campaign.
Whether the fund raiser be on a small or large scale, there will always be
a "plan of attack". This plan will usually start with invitations and also
include a special attraction, entertainment and refreshments. From the
beginning to the end of the fund raiser, steps will be followed. Auditing
these fund raisers requires knowledge of the planning steps involved and
an insight of problems that might materialize. During the audit, the pro-
blems encountered center on internal control. Only through regulations or
guidelines for campaign fund raisers can an auditor be assured that certain
steps were taken to guarantee control over a fund raising event.

          It is the consensus of the group that in most cases internal control is
weak in relation to a fund raising event. There is usually no control over
the contributions received prior to or during the fund raiser. Nor is there
control over the number of people in attendance and in relation to the amount
of contributions received. In the area of nonmonetary contributions, there
is usually no record kept. It is difficult for an auditor to determine possi-
bilities of nonmonetary contributions without any indication as to the type
of fund raiser given.

          In regard to expenditures incurred, it is difficult to relate these expenses
with the fund raising event. It is to the auditor's advantage if a relation-
ship between the expenses and the event can be established. This relationship
would give the auditor an insight to nonmonetary contributions and expenses
paid by another party.

          In the case where another committee, whether it is a subcommittee or a se-
parate entity, gives a fund raising event on behalf of a candidate there may
or may not be any control over such event by the candidate's controlled commit-
tee. In this instance, a separate bank account could be established which
would permit nondisclosure of contributions and expenditures.

          These problems can be minimized through regulations or guidelines for campaign
fund raisers. As part of a recordkeeping guideline, committees should be re-
quired to keep a record of each fund raiser. In addition, the date, type and

Mark Ford
Michele Holm
Jerry Richard
Page 2
May 24, 1977


committee members involved with the fund raiser should be recorded.
This requirement would aid the auditor in third party contacts and give
her/him an idea of the scope and depth of the event.

Although expenditures and contributions over $50 are already disclosed,
there should be an indication either on the report or in the records if
the expenses or the contributions relate to a fund raiser. Nonmonetary
contributions or expenses paid by a third party could be easily detected
if correspondence among expenses, contributions and fund raisers can be
identified.

Control throughout the fund raiser is essential. To insure control it
should be present in the first stages of planning. There should be control
over the number of invitations sent or tickets sold prior to or during the
event. Also a correlation should be attained between the number of people
who contributed with either the response cards from invitations or number
of tickets sold. In cases where there are "cash barrels" at the door,
someone should be responsible for logging in the amount received per person.
This would curtail any cash contributions of $50 or more.

In the cases where separate entities sponsor a fund raiser for a candidate,
they too, should be subject to the internal control requirements. In addition, the separate entity should secure authorization from the candidate
or her/his controlled committee to give the event. A record of the event
should be kept by the controlled committee.

In no way would the auditors want too stringent requirements that would
eventually inhibit the campaign process. To meet this end our means must
be reasonable and sound. Initially, there should be an understanding that
these requirements are for the campaign's protection and that the steps
taken to insure control would only expedite the audit.

Carmella J. Tamayo

Auditor
Coastal Audit Group 01

sections.question_synthetic: This document is not a direct legal question but rather internal audit division memoranda discussing audit problems, procedures, and recommendations related to campaign finance reporting requirements for mass mailings and fund raisers.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document is a collection of internal audit division memoranda rather than an FPPC advice letter response to an external request. No traditional QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Multiple memos from Franchise Tax Board staff describing audit challenges, procedures, and policy recommendations. Some OCR quality issues with character recognition in older document portions.

embedding.summary: A series of internal FPPC Political Reform Audit Division memoranda from May-June 1977 documenting audit problems encountered during campaign audits, particularly regarding mass mailings and fund raisers, along with recommended audit procedures and suggestions for improved recordkeeping regulations and guidelines.
